title: Writing - Part 1: Universal Basic Income
description: Your class has attended a debate on whether governments should introduce a Universal Basic Income (UBI)—a regular payment made to every citizen regardless of employment status.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the arguments in your notes. You should explain which argument you find most compelling, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: A basic income floor would eliminate the very worst poverty.
People may simply stop working if income is guaranteed regardless.
Funding universal basic income would require crippling levels of taxation.

structure.introduction: Introduce UBI as a radical policy proposal and the fierce debate it provokes.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Examine the potential of UBI to reduce poverty and provide genuine economic security.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the concern that UBI would undermine the incentive to work and be prohibitively expensive.
structure.conclusion: Conclude by stating which argument is more persuasive and whether you believe UBI is a viable policy.

content.modelAnswer:
Universal Basic Income—the proposal to provide every citizen with an unconditional regular payment from the state—has moved from the fringes of political debate to serious policy consideration in recent years. Its proponents see it as a transformative response to inequality and automation; its critics regard it as economically reckless. This essay will examine both the poverty reduction argument and the concern about cost and labour market effects.

The most compelling case for UBI rests on its potential to eradicate poverty in a fundamentally dignified way. Unlike means-tested benefits, which are frequently stigmatised, administratively complex, and prone to creating 'poverty traps' that discourage recipients from seeking employment, a universal payment treats every citizen equally. Pilot programmes conducted in Finland, Kenya, and several Canadian provinces suggest that recipients do not, in fact, withdraw from work en masse; they use the security it provides to take greater risks, pursue education, and move into more meaningful employment.

Nevertheless, the fiscal challenges are formidable. Providing a meaningful income to every adult citizen of a large economy would require enormous funding, and the tax increases necessary to finance it could suppress economic activity and incentivise capital flight. Critics also argue that if the payment were set too low to provide genuine security, it would be an expensive irrelevance; if set high enough to matter, it would be unsustainable.

In conclusion, I find the poverty reduction argument ultimately more persuasive. The evidence from pilots suggests that fears about work incentives are exaggerated, and the social cost of persistent poverty arguably exceeds the fiscal challenge of addressing it.
